Final Step: Narrowing Down to Just Five Companies

In this step, we construct three separate lists of five stocks each, with different sets of goals, dividend income, and risk levels.

The lists are:

1) Relatively Safe (Low-yield) Dividend list,

2) Moderately High Dividend List,

3) Ultra High Dividend List, and

4) A combined list of the above three (duplicates removed).

Out of the top 40, we make our judgment calls to make these three lists, so basically, the selections are based on our research and perceptions. So, while most of the filtering until now was based on automated criteria, the last step is pretty much subjective. We try to make each of the three lists highly diversified among various sectors and industry segments and try to ensure that the safety of dividends matches the overall risk profile of the group. We certainly encourage readers to do further research on the highlighted names.

Nonetheless, here are our three final lists for this month:

Final A-List (Relatively Safe Income):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 2.75% and 4.25%.
  • At least three or four names with an "A" rating (from S&P)
  • Solid track record of dividends and growth.

Average yield: 3.88%

  • (MSFT)
  • (ADP)
  • (BX)
  • (CVX)
  • (PFE)

Table-1A: A-LIST (Conservative Income)

We think this set of five companies (in the A-List) would form a solid, diversified group of dividend companies that would be appealing to income-seeking and conservative investors, including retirees and near-retirees. The average yield is 3.88%, which is more than 3x of what the S&P 500 pays. The average dividend history is 33 years. Also, all five companies have an excellent rating of "A-" or higher. If you must have higher dividends, consider the B-List or the C-List, presented later.

As usual, we will provide a brief synopsis of each of the companies selected here; however, we will not go into too many details, as that will make the article unduly long.

MSFT (Microsoft)

MSFT is currently trading at nearly 25% below its 52-week high. In fact, it has lost over 17% since the beginning of 2026. This year, the software stocks are having a terrible time due to AI fears. However, Microsoft is too diversified and entrenched in the enterprise processes. To think that it will be replaced by AI any time soon is too pessimistic a view. In fact, Microsoft, with its massive investments in OpenAI, is likely to benefit from the AI trends. Also, the company is one of only three large public cloud providers and can provide software solutions to enterprises at scale. The company's Azure business is still growing at nearly 30% annually. All that said, the stock price had gotten ahead of itself last year, and the current prices offer a great opportunity to own this company at lower prices. The yield is still very low at 0.93%, but we are including this company in the A-list for dividend growth and capital appreciation rather than yield.

ADP (Automatic Data Processing)

ADP's yield is moderate but has gone up considerably in recent times due to an ongoing price correction. The current dividend yield of 3.17% is very favorable compared to its 5-year average of 2%. It is often considered a favorite of dividend investors, given the company's stability and the reliability of the dividend and above-average dividend growth. It is relatively rare to find this company trading at cheaper valuations, except during deep market corrections. However, due to panic selling of software stocks due to perceived fear of AI takeover, it offers a unique opportunity. Investors have sold off their software stocks, irrespective of the quality. There is some argument that advancements in AI could result in improved capabilities of corporations to move payroll processing in-house, thereby impacting ADP's business. However, the fear is overdone and ignores the company's potential to use AI effectively to increase its business and reach.

Morningstar rates the stock price as undervalued by more than 25%, and the share price is nearly 35% below its 52-week high.

CVX (Chevron)

CVX frequently takes a spot on our A-list due to its relatively safe, moderately high dividend and excellent credit rating. Even though the energy sector is usually volatile, CVX is probably one of the best investments in its sector for stable income and reasonable returns over time. Chevron is known to be a low-cost producer with its breakeven price in the low to mid-40s. The company maintains an excellent credit rating of "AA" from S&P, which shows its financial strength.

However, recently, the energy sector has been gaining momentum, and especially due to the evolving geopolitical situation, oil prices and the company's stock price have gained considerably.

CVX currently offers an attractive dividend yield of 3.8%, but it is considerably lower than what it was last year. It is best to accumulate this stock over a period of time and take the benefit of dollar cost averaging.

PFE (Pfizer):

We are including Pfizer in all three lists this month. The reason is obviously the attractive and tempting yield of 6.2%. We always tend to include one company in the A-list with a slightly higher yield. However, we try to minimize the risks.

The health and pharma sector was highly undervalued in 2025 due to many ongoing uncertainties, but those uncertainties are gradually fading away. This has been reflected in the recovery of the sector in 2026. This has benefited Pfizer's stock price to some extent, and it has gained nearly 2525 from the lows of 2025. However, the prices and the yield are still attractive. In fact, the yield is still the highest amongst the major pharma companies, but the dividend growth is tepid. Further, Morningstar assigns it a 4-star rating, and the current price is nearly 20% below its estimate of fair value. The company enjoys an excellent credit rating, even though the acquisition of Seagen in 2023 and Metsera in 2025 added to Pfizer's debt.

Pfizer's pipeline has been improving with several successful recent drug launches and acquisitions. There will be some patent losses between 2026 and 2028, but the major ones will be towards the end of 2028. So, the company has ample time and potential to replace them with the new drugs.

BX (Blackstone Inc.):

Blackstone is one of the most respected alternative-asset managers. It has roughly $1.2 trillion in AUM (assets under management), out of which roughly over 72% generate fee-based revenue. Its major business/product lines include private equity, real estate (or real assets), private credit, and other alternatives. Blackstone has specialized in revitalizing property assets or companies by various means. Even though fundraising by alternative asset managers was challenging during 2022-2025 due to elevated interest rates, Blackstone was able to raise $150 billion or more in each of the past three years.

The company's shares have lost roughly 25% since the beginning of this year, mostly due to adverse industry-wide cyclical headwinds. That said, the company has faced such issues before, and due to its scale and management excellence, it has come out stronger each time. So, it may be a good time to open a position. In the meantime, the company generates a lot of fee-based revenue and pays over 80% of that in the form of dividends. The current dividend yield is 5.26%, but the dividend amount can vary greatly from year to year. The dividend growth has been patchy, but it has still grown 13% annualized in the last 5-6 years (since 2020). Morningstar assigns it a wide-moat rating in its industry and assigns a fair value nearly 35% above its current price.

Final B-List (High Yield, Moderately Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • Yield for the group between 4.75% and 6.50%.
  • At least two names with an 'A-' (or above) rating.
  • Above-average track record of dividends and growth.

Average yield: 6.68%

  • (ADP)
  • (MPLX)
  • (GLPI)
  • (ARCC)
  • (PFE)

Note 1: Very often, we include a few low-risk stocks in the B-list and C-list. Also, oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. This is done on purpose. We try to make each of our lists fairly diversified among different sectors/industry segments of the economy. We try to include a few of the highly conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1B: B-LIST (High Yield)

NOTE: MPLX is structured as a partnership (not a corporation) and issues a K-1 tax form instead of the usual 1099-Div. Please use due diligence.

=====

In the B-List, the overall risk profile of the group becomes slightly elevated compared to the A-List. That said, the group (as a whole) will likely provide safe dividends for many years. This list offers an average yield of 6.7%, an average of 26 years of dividend history, and 12 years (average) of consecutive growth.

Some of our high-yield selections often repeat in the B-list and C-list.

MPLX (MPLX LP)

MPLX LP makes it to our B-list and C-list on a regular basis. It is a highly attractive name from the midstream energy industry. Please note that the company has a Partnership structure and issues a K-1 tax form (partnership income) instead of the usual 1099 in the case of regular dividends.

We have covered MPLX multiple times in our previous articles in this monthly series. Hence, we will skip the details here. MPLX regularly features on our B-list and C-list due to its high income, stable business, and decent dividend growth. There are other good alternatives, such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) or Energy Transfer (ET).

ARCC (Ares Capital)

ARCC is one of the largest BDCs (Business Development Companies) in terms of market capitalization and net asset value. The company invests in small and medium-sized companies and has a diversified portfolio of investments in over 500 companies. Besides, ARCC has a proven record of performing reasonably well both in high- and low-interest-rate environments. The company offers a stable dividend. The current environment is somewhat challenging, to say the least, and that's why the valuation has come down significantly and offers a good opportunity for long-term investors.

It generates a solid amount of NII (net investment income), which currently exceeds well above the $1.2 billion mark on an annual basis. ARCC currently offers a highly attractive yield of roughly 10.3%. The most recent NII for the last quarter was $0.52 a share, which was still above the quarterly dividend of $0.48 a share, though the margin of coverage is much lower.

GLPI (Gaming and Leisure Properties):

GLPI is a real estate investment property trust, and it specializes in acquiring, financing, owning, and leasing real estate properties related to the gaming and leisure industry. GLPI leases its properties to gaming operators in triple-net lease arrangements.

GLPI came into existence as a REIT in 2013 due to the spinoff from Penn National Gaming. Since then, it has continually expanded its operations at a steady pace and now owns a portfolio of 69 gaming properties with over 15,000 hotel rooms spanning across 20 states. Most GLPI properties are in the northeastern and south-central parts of the United States. Its portfolio is considered high quality, with several tenants ranked number one in their respective markets. The only other publicly traded REIT in the gaming industry is VICI Properties (VICI), which is much larger than GLPI in terms of its assets and market cap.

GLPI's occupancy rates have been extremely good at 100% for most of its existence. It has a total of eight unique tenants; however, over 87% of the rents are received from just four tenants/operators. So, in that sense, it is rather tenant-concentrated, but it has had zero rent defaults so far. Also, this is just the nature of the gaming industry, as there are only a limited number of operators.

The company is currently paying a dividend of $0.78 per share, which increased from $0.76 per share in the year-ago period (and $0.73 two years ago). So, its dividend is $3.12 a year, and the AFFO in 2025 was $3.88, covering the dividend at 1.24x. The AFFO guidance for 2026 is between $4.06 and $4.11.

GLPI is a very stable cash-flow generator and a reliable dividend payer. With the exception of 2020, when it cut the dividends by about 10%, it has raised the dividends every year. It also pays a small amount in special dividends sometimes. The average dividend growth has been in the low single digits over the last 5 years and 10 years. The current yield is very attractive at 6.4% and slightly better than its five-year average of roughly 6%.

Note: We would like to caution that each company comes with certain risks and concerns. Sometimes, these risks are real, but other times, they may be a bit overblown and temporary. So, it's always recommended to do further research and due diligence.

Final C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Less Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 7.0% and 8.0%
  • All names with investment-grade ratings.
  • Some potential for growth.

Average yield: 7.50%

  • (MPLX)
  • (GLPI)
  • (ARCC)
  • (PFE)
  • (CAG)

Notes:

Note 1: Oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. We try to include one or two conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1C: C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Elevated Risk)

NOTE: MPLX is structured as a partnership (not a corporation) and issues a K-1 tax form instead of the usual 1099-Div. Please use due diligence.

=====

It is apparent from the selections that this list (C-List) is for yield-hungry DGI investors. The yield goes up to 7.50%. However, please note that this list is not for conservative investors. As you can see, the average credit rating of this set of companies is much lower than the A-List or even the B-List. Dividends are moderately safe in this list, but not nearly as safe as in the A-List or even the B-list. At least a couple of companies in this list come with an elevated level of risk, as they are turnaround stories. We urge due diligence to determine if it would suit your personal situation. Nothing comes for free, so there will be more risk involved with this group. Even then, it's a diversified group spread among five different industry segments.

CAG (Conagra Brands)

Conagra offers a very attractive dividend yield of 7.3%. At first, we should be alarmed by that level of dividend yield from a consumer staples company. So, apparently, there are risks that investors should be aware of before they make investments in this company. The company's stock price has been in a constant downtrend all of this year and has lost more than 50% since 2023. The stock has lost over 33% in 2025 alone, while the S&P 500 gained 19% in the same time period. However, that is in the past, and most of the downside appears to be priced in, and further downside may be limited, if any.

Now, some negatives and risks. The company does not enjoy any moat and is facing stiff competition and pricing pressures from private labels. Moreover, its balance sheet is not in a very strong situation, with net debt/adjusted-EBITDA of nearly 3.8x. Also, the dividend payout is somewhat elevated at 73%; however, the company is targeting to bring it down to 55% in the next few years. With that outlook, there is high income with potentially some risks. Only income investors with high-risk tolerance should consider this stock.

What if We Were to Combine the Three Lists?

If we combine the three lists and remove the duplicates (because of combining), we would be left with nine names.

Two-time duplicates: ADP, ARCC, GLPI, and MPLX (4 duplicates).

Three-time duplicates: PFE (2 duplicates).

After removing these SIX duplicates, we are left with nine names.

The combined list is highly diversified in as many as nine different industry segments. However, there may be multiple names for some of the sectors. The stats for the group of 9 are as follows:

Average yield: 5.63%

Average discount (from 52WK High): -17.5%

Average 5-yr dividend growth: 7%.

Average Payout Ratio: 72%.

Average Total Quality Score: 64.3.

Table 3:

Conclusion:

In this article, we have presented three groups of dividend-paying stocks (five each) with different goals in mind to suit the varying needs of a wider audience. Even though the risk profile of each group is different, each group in itself is fairly balanced and diversified.

This month, the first group yields 3.88%, while the second group elevates the yield to 6.68%. We also presented a C-list for yield-hungry investors with a 7.50% yield. The combined group (all three lists combined with duplication removed) offers an even more diversified group with nine positions and a 5.63% yield.

data.title: RadNet's Pullback Offers Upside With Outpatient Imaging And AI
data.description:
RadNet, Inc. (RDNT) is a healthcare company that provides outpatient imaging services. Its Digital Health segment, DeepHealth, offers workflow software and clinical AI for radiology operations. The company grows through joint ventures with hospitals, new centers, and acquisitions. Recently, RDNT acquired Gleamer, which is an AI-powered radiology company that will become part of its DeepHealth portfolio. And overall, I believe RDNT trades at compelling valuation multiples in light of its growth prospects (and secular AI trends), which is why I deem the stock a “Strong Buy” at these levels.

Promising Diagnostic Imaging Business

RadNet, Inc. is a healthcare company that provides diagnostic imaging services. It operates a large network of facilities, comprising 418 centers across the US, reporting the completion of more than 10 million outpatient imaging procedures annually. RDNT was founded back in 1981 and is currently headquartered in Los Angeles, California. However, they recently reported record revenues and completed a major AI acquisition, which I believe makes it a very interesting stock to cover.

You see, as a quick recap, RDNT’s Imaging Center Segment offers services such as MRI for soft tissue and CT for cross-sectional body images. Similarly, they provide PET scans that show activity in the body. RDNT’s centers also provide routine imaging like nuclear medicine for organ function, mammography, ultrasound, X-rays, fluoroscopy, and related procedures. Most centers offer several types of imaging in one location for convenience, so it’s a well-rounded business already in this niche.

Moreover, RDNT also has a Digital Health segment that develops and sells software and clinical applications to support imaging operations and interpretation. This includes eRAD, which offers software like radiology information systems (RIS) and picture archiving and communication systems (PACS). The segment also features DeepHealth, which uses AI technology.

In that sense, RDNT’s DeepHealth products are cloud-native imaging informatics with tools that help standardize and provide enhanced reading and management of imaging studies. This technology offers services for cancer screening, allowing for early detection of the disease. These products are also useful for managing risk and follow-ups across population groups systematically. And more importantly, it’s a segment that could benefit greatly from AI as well, which I believe adds compelling optionality to RDNT stock as a whole.

Strongest Quarter, Winning Acquisitions

In fact, RDNT’s services are already showing a rapidly increasing utilization rate because payers and patients prefer lower-cost outpatient imaging. This is a much more affordable alternative relative to traditional hospital-based imaging methods. Plus, RDNT captures demand that moves out of hospitals and into more personalized healthcare services. Management stated that Q4 2025 was their strongest in the company’s history, with a record revenue of $547.7 million. The aggregate volume growth was 14.1%, including existing and newly acquired sites, as well as new builds. Similarly, advanced imaging procedural volume grew by 9.6%, showcasing that the core business is healthy as well.

Furthermore, RDNT's advanced imaging services, like MRI, CT, and PET, generated more than 60% of revenue. And out of all scans done, 28.6% corresponded to these higher-value exams. These results benefit from an ongoing industry-wide trend, where doctors order more of these scans due to the advances in imaging technology. RDNT offers updated scanners to increase capacity and replace old equipment, and their AI-powered software helps optimize scheduling, which is a nice differentiator with healthcare providers and patients alike.

Currently, 151 of 418 RDNT’s centers are joint ventures with hospitals. During 2025, RDNT expanded its joint venture partnerships with 7 centers built and other acquisitions. The Digital Health acquisitions were iCAD, See-Mode, and CIMAR, integrated into the DeepHealth portfolio. In January, RDNT acquired 13 centers in Southwest Florida (Radiology Regional), 6 centers in Indiana (Northwest Radiology), and one center in Virginia.

However, recently RDNT completed a more transformative acquisition with Gleamer. This was a Paris-based radiology artificial intelligence company that will be folded into RDNT’s DeepHealth segment. The deal was all-cash with a post-closing milestone, up to a €230 million purchase price. Gleamer is strong in routine imaging, especially X-ray. However, its combined suite also spans MR, CT, mammography, and ultrasound. And more importantly, has a great strategic fit within DeepHealth due to its AI know-how in radiology, which will likely also be integrated into RDNT’s core outpatient imaging services.

This matters because Gleamer’s acquisition brings FDA-approved and CE-marked devices/software. So, RDNT’s software will now include AI capabilities that flag studies with potentially urgent findings, so they are put at the top of the reading list. Also, RDNT’s AI generates a first-pass report that a radiologist reviews, edits, and signs, improving the speed and consistency of the reports. The software is primarily delivered through the cloud as a SaaS model, with customers paying ongoing subscription fees. Going forward, RDNT expects that the subscription revenue run rate related to this SaaS model will be around $30 million. However, I believe that over time, this alone could become a major revenue pillar for them.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, RDNT currently trades at a $5.2 billion market cap. Their 2025 10-K report shows its balance sheet holds $767.2 million in cash and equivalents against approximately $1.1 billion in debt (aside from other regular operating liabilities). In total, its book value reached $1.4 billion, and I calculate its EV is around $5.5 billion. It’s also worth mentioning that RDNT is projected to generate $2.7 billion in revenues by 2027, which would price it at a reasonable forward EV/S of 2.0. For comparison, its sector’s median forward EV/S is closer to 3.3, so I do believe RDNT may still have room to run at these levels.

Moreover, remember that management’s latest guidance was also remarkably optimistic. They generated $300.2 million in adjusted-EBITDA during 2025, which is a good proxy for cash flow. And in particular, they anticipate that adjusted-EBITDA from their Imaging Center segment will grow another 18%-22% in 2026, which I think clearly shows RDNT will have more than enough cash flows to fund their AI bets and operations. Plus, it would also imply an EV/EBITDA of 18.3. While this is a bit more expensive than its peers’ EV/EBITDA of 14.0, management’s projected adjusted-EBITDA growth rate seems to significantly outpace the sector’s forward EBITDA growth of 9.1%. Thus, I do feel it’s a justified premium valuation for RDNT.

Alternatively, I also calculate that RDNT generated around $85.5 million in positive cash flow during 2025. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its 2025 cash flows from operations and CAPEX. And, while this is a more conservative representation of their cash flows at this point, it nonetheless corroborates that RDNT has a successful underlying model and solid balance, which further derisks their investment thesis today.

Having said that, RDNT’s risk profile is now mostly centered around how well management executes its ongoing strategies. For example, if their revenue growth fails to materialize, or maybe the Gleamer acquisition takes longer to fully integrate, then we could see some multiple contraction for the stock. Similarly, RDNT’s AI bets may not pay off as management expects, and this is not entirely out of the picture since AI is an extremely rapidly evolving and shifting trend. In my view, any of these types of mishaps could prompt an unfavorable repricing of the stock, but I nevertheless remain optimistic since management has delivered on its promises consistently so far.

Conclusion: Great Risk-Reward

Overall, RDNT strikes me as the type of company you want to buy and hold for the long run. It’s a successful business with a healthy balance sheet and rapidly growing cash flows that’s betting on major secular trends. The stock is now also 28% down from its 2025 highs, and the valuation multiples seem compelling when you consider its growth prospects into 2026 and beyond. Naturally, there are always unforeseen events that could derail this bull case, especially since a lot of RDNT’s growth strategy hinges on AI, which adds uncertainty to its otherwise stellar prospects. But, on balance, I believe RDNT is a great “Strong Buy” for investors at these levels who believe in this management team and outpatient imaging business model.

data.title: EastGroup Properties: A Quiet Compounder Delivering Market-Beating Returns
data.description:
Owning hard assets simply makes sense for those who prize cash flow and easy-to-understand business models. It now matters more than ever, especially as SaaS companies have come under pressure due to concerns around AI disruption.

Geopolitical conflicts also make the case for owning assets that are homegrown in the U.S., generating cash flows that aren’t subject to currency translation risk for U.S. investors.

This brings me to EastGroup Properties (EGP), which I last covered in January 2025, highlighting its focus on high-demand, supply-constrained markets and its attractive valuation. It appears my ‘buy’ thesis has paid off, with the stock delivering a 23% total return since my last piece, outperforming the 15% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY). Who says you have to buy popular stocks in the tech sector to get market-beating returns?

In this article, I revisit EGP, including recent business results, and discuss why it’s a ‘Buy’ for conservative income investors seeking long-term capital appreciation, so let’s get started!

Why EGP?

EastGroup Properties is a REIT that’s focused on the development and acquisition of industrial properties in high-growth Sunbelt markets. It specializes in “shallow-bay” business distribution facilities, which are multi-tenant buildings typically ranging from roughly 20K to 100K square feet. Its properties serve last-mile logistics, light manufacturing, and service businesses.

Shallow bay properties tend to have better operating fundamentals compared to big box warehouses. This is due to the fungible nature of the properties that cater to a wider variety of tenants. This has also enabled EGP to sustain high occupancy and consistent FFO growth over time. As shown below, shallow bay vacancy has been on average lower than big box properties over the past 17 years.

EGP demonstrated solid Q4 2025 results, with FFO per share rising by 8.8% YoY for the quarter and by 7.7% for the full year. This marks over 10 years of FFO per share growth, signaling the durability of EGP’s business model across business cycles. Occupancy remained strong at 96.2%, marking a 40 basis point improvement on a sequential basis. Notably, same-store occupancy was higher at 97.4%, while newer developments are still in the lease-up stage.

Importantly, EGP is seeing robust rent growth, with re-leasing spreads reaching 19% and 35% on a cash and GAAP basis, respectively, during the quarter. This contributed to strong full-year results, with re-leasing spreads of 25% and 40% on a cash and GAAP basis, respectively. This contributed to strong same-store NOI growth of 8.4% and 6.7% for Q4’25 and the full year.

Looking ahead, development remains the cornerstone of EGP’s growth strategy due to the healthy spread between development yields and acquisition cap rates. Currently, the spread is around 190 basis points, with development yields sitting above 7% and acquisition yields in the low to mid-5% cap rate range. EGP is expecting $250 million in development starts this year, with projects being largely driven by tenant demand within existing industrial parks.

The pipeline of potential tenants is also encouraging, with EGP in discussion with multiple prospects. Notably, tenant demand is coming from a mix of sources, including relocations from higher-cost markets like California. At the same time, new supply coming online is expected to be the lowest since 2018, as shown below.

This is due to limited new development space, where zoning restrictions and permitting challenges are making it harder for developers to bring new projects online. EGP, on the other hand, has a substantial land bank covering 1,000 acres with permits already in place in many cases. This allows it to quickly accelerate projects in response to tenant demand ahead of the competition.

EGP carries a fortress balance sheet with a low debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 3x. Its debt to total capitalization sits at only 15%, and it has a very high fixed charge coverage ratio of over 15x. EGP currently yields a respectable 3.3%. The dividend is well protected by a 67% payout ratio and has a 5-year CAGR of 14%.

Admittedly, EGP is no longer cheap at the current price of $188 with a forward P/FFO of 19.7. However, it’s far from being expensive, either, relative to its recent and forward growth outlook.

Given its strong re-leasing rates and development pipeline, I expect EGP to be able to deliver 7-9% annual FFO/share growth in the medium term. This, combined with the dividend, offers investors potential total returns in the low teens, all with a far higher yield and dividend growth rate than the market average.

Risks to EGP include development risks such as cost overruns or construction delays, which could lower the initial yield on these projects. Slower than expected leasing on these projects could also lower the forward growth outlook. Plus, macroeconomic pressures stemming from higher gas prices could result in an industrial slowdown and lower tenant demand in the near term.

Investor Takeaway

EastGroup Properties remains a solid option for investors seeking dependable income and steady long-term growth from a straightforward business model. Its focus on shallow-bay industrial properties in high-growth Sunbelt markets continues to produce strong operating results. This includes high occupancy, robust rent spreads, and more than a decade of consistent FFO per-share growth.

EGP is positioned to benefit from limited new supply, a sizable and permitted land bank, and an attractive spread between development yields and acquisition cap rates.

The stock is no longer deeply discounted. However, EGP’s fortress balance sheet, well-covered 3.3% yield, and visible development pipeline support the case for low-teens total return potential. With valuation sitting higher now than my prior visit, I’m downgrading the stock from a ‘Strong Buy’ to a ‘Buy.’
